question: What is semantic HTML and why does it matter?
answer:
Using elements that describe their meaning — <header>, <nav>, <main>, <article>, <footer> — instead of generic <div>s.

Benefits: better accessibility (screen readers understand structure), improved SEO, and more maintainable markup.
tip: Reach for a div only when no semantic element fits.

question: What does <!DOCTYPE html> do?
answer: It tells the browser to render in standards mode rather than legacy 'quirks mode'. Without it, older browsers emulate 1990s bugs and box models, breaking layouts.
tip: It's a mode switch, not an HTML tag or a version declaration.

question: Block vs inline vs inline-block elements.
answer:
• Block — starts on a new line, takes full width (div, p, section).
  • Inline — flows within text, ignores width/height (span, a).
  • inline-block — flows inline but respects width/height/margins.
tip: You can't set width/height on a pure inline element — switch to inline-block.

question: What does the viewport meta tag do?
answer: It controls how mobile browsers scale the page. Without it, phones render at desktop width and zoom out. width=device-width, initial-scale=1 makes the layout match the device's real width — essential for responsive design.
tip: Forget this tag and your media queries won't kick in on phones.

question: What are data-* attributes?
answer: Custom attributes for storing app data on an element without polluting standard attributes. Read them in JS via the dataset API.
tip: Great for event delegation — stash an id on the row and read e.target.dataset.id.

question: localStorage vs sessionStorage vs cookies.
answer:
• localStorage — ~5–10MB, persists until cleared, not sent to the server.
  • sessionStorage — same API, cleared when the tab closes.
  • cookies — small (~4KB), sent with every HTTP request; used for auth/session tokens.
tip: Never store JWTs in localStorage if you can avoid it — httpOnly cookies resist XSS.

question: script defer vs async.
answer:
Both download the script without blocking HTML parsing.

  • async — runs as soon as it's downloaded, order not guaranteed. For independent scripts (analytics).
  • defer — runs after parsing, in order. For app scripts that depend on the DOM or each other.
tip: Use defer for your app bundle so it runs after the DOM is ready and in order.

question: What native form validation does HTML5 provide?
answer: Attributes like required, type="email", min/max, pattern, and minlength validate input before submit, with built-in error UI — no JS needed for basics.
tip: Always validate again on the server — client validation is a UX nicety, not security.

question: How do you make a page accessible?
answer:
• Semantic elements + correct heading order.
  • alt text on images; labels tied to inputs.
  • Keyboard operability and visible focus states.
  • ARIA roles/attributes only when native elements can't express the semantics.
  • Sufficient colour contrast.
tip: First rule of ARIA: don't use ARIA if a native element already does the job.

question: How do you serve responsive images?
answer: Use srcset + sizes so the browser picks the best resolution for the device, or <picture> for art direction / modern formats (WebP/AVIF) with fallbacks. Add loading="lazy" for offscreen images.
tip: loading="lazy" is a one-attribute performance win for image-heavy pages.

question: Which meta tags matter for SEO and sharing?
answer: <title> and <meta name="description"> for search results; Open Graph (og:title, og:image) and Twitter Card tags for rich link previews; canonical to avoid duplicate-content penalties.
tip: og:image is what makes your link look good when shared on WhatsApp/LinkedIn.

question: What makes a site a PWA?
answer: Three ingredients: a web app manifest (name, icons, theme, display mode), a service worker (offline caching + install), and HTTPS. Together they let the site be installed to the home screen and work offline.
tip: A manifest + service worker + HTTPS is the minimum for the 'Install' prompt.

question: What are Web Components?
answer: A set of browser standards for reusable, framework-agnostic UI: Custom Elements (define your own tags), Shadow DOM (encapsulated markup + styles), and HTML templates (<template>/<slot>). Angular can render as custom elements via Angular Elements.
tip: Shadow DOM gives true style encapsulation — the browser-native version of Angular's emulated encapsulation.

question: Why do input types matter (email, number, tel, date)?
answer: The right type gives free validation, the correct mobile keyboard (numeric/email layout), native pickers (date/color), and better accessibility — improving UX with zero JS.
tip: type="email" and type="tel" change the phone keyboard — a quick mobile UX win.

question: What does the native <dialog> element give you?
answer: A built-in modal/popup with showModal()/close(), automatic focus trapping, backdrop (::backdrop), and Esc-to-close — accessibility that used to require a library.
tip: Use <dialog> before reaching for a modal library — it's accessible by default.

question: What are <details> and <summary>?
answer: A native, zero-JS disclosure widget: <summary> is the always-visible label and the rest of <details> expands/collapses on click — accessible and keyboard-friendly out of the box (this app's cards use the same idea).
tip: Perfect for FAQs and accordions without any JavaScript.

question: Why add rel="noopener" to target="_blank" links?
answer: Without it, the newly opened page can access window.opener and hijack the original tab (tabnabbing). rel="noopener" severs that reference; noreferrer also hides the referrer. Modern browsers imply noopener, but set it explicitly.
tip: Always pair target="_blank" with rel="noopener" for external links.

question: preload vs prefetch vs preconnect.
answer:
• preload — fetch a critical resource for the current page early (fonts, hero image).
  • prefetch — low-priority fetch of a resource likely needed on the next navigation.
  • preconnect — warm up the connection (DNS/TLS) to a third-party origin.
tip: preload for 'need it now', prefetch for 'probably need it next'.

question: Canvas vs SVG — when to use which?
answer: SVG is vector, DOM-based, scalable, and stylable/accessible — good for icons, charts, and anything interactive or resolution-independent. Canvas is an immediate-mode pixel bitmap — better for lots of objects, games, and heavy pixel manipulation.
tip: Few interactive shapes → SVG; thousands of pixels/particles → Canvas.

question: What does tabindex do?
answer:
• tabindex="0" — makes a non-focusable element focusable in normal order.
  • tabindex="-1" — focusable via script only (not Tab), for managing focus.
  • tabindex="1+" — avoid; it breaks natural tab order.
tip: Prefer native interactive elements (button/a) over div + tabindex.

question: What is the Critical Rendering Path?
answer: The steps the browser takes to turn HTML/CSS/JS into pixels: build the DOM and CSSOM, combine into the render tree, then layout and paint. CSS is render-blocking and synchronous JS is parser-blocking — optimise by inlining critical CSS, deferring JS, and minimising above-the-fold work.
tip: Render-blocking CSS + parser-blocking JS are the two levers for faster first paint.

question: What is an ARIA live region?
answer: An element with aria-live that tells screen readers to announce dynamic content changes (toasts, validation errors, search-result counts) without moving focus. polite waits for a pause; assertive interrupts.
tip: Wrap a search-results count in aria-live="polite" so it's announced as it updates.

question: What is the DOM?
answer:
The Document Object Model is the browser's in-memory, tree-shaped representation of your HTML. Each tag becomes a node, and JavaScript can read and change these nodes (querySelector, textContent, createElement) to update the page dynamically.

The DOM is a live API, not the original HTML source — frameworks like Angular/React manipulate it (often via a virtual DOM or their own diffing) instead of you touching it by hand.
tip: HTML is the source text; the DOM is the live object tree the browser builds from it.

question: Form method GET vs POST — what's the difference?
answer:
• GET — appends form data to the URL as query params. Visible, bookmarkable, cached, length-limited. Use for searches/filters (idempotent reads).
  • POST — sends data in the request body. Not in the URL, not cached, no size limit. Use for creating/changing data (logins, submissions).

Never send passwords or sensitive data with GET — it lands in URLs, history, and server logs.
tip: GET = read/idempotent (safe to repeat); POST = write (changes server state).

question: What is the difference between id and class?
answer:
• id uniquely identifies a single element on the page and should only be used once.
  • class can be reused across multiple elements.
  • CSS Specificity: #id has higher specificity than .class.
  • JavaScript: Access an id using getElementById(), while classes are commonly selected using querySelectorAll().
tip: Use classes for styling and reuse. Reserve ids for unique elements or anchor links.

question: Why should you use the label element with the for attribute?
answer: The label element improves accessibility by associating descriptive text with an input. Clicking the label automatically focuses the corresponding input, making forms easier to use for both mouse and keyboard users as well as screen readers.
tip: Every form input should have an associated label whenever possible.

question: Why is the alt attribute important for images?
answer: The alt attribute provides alternative text when an image cannot be displayed. It is also read by screen readers for visually impaired users and helps search engines understand image content. Decorative images should use alt="".
tip: Don't use the file name as alt text. Describe the image's purpose instead.

question: What is an iframe and what security precautions should you take?
answer: An iframe embeds another webpage inside the current page. It is commonly used for videos, maps, and third-party widgets. Since embedded content may be untrusted, use the sandbox attribute to restrict its capabilities and improve security.
tip: Always apply the minimum permissions required when using the sandbox attribute.

question: What is the autocomplete attribute and why is it useful?
answer: The autocomplete attribute tells the browser whether it should automatically fill form fields using previously saved user information. It improves user experience by reducing typing and supports values like email, username, current-password, and new-password.
tip: Use meaningful autocomplete values instead of simply turning autocomplete off.
code:
<input type="email" autocomplete="email">
<input type="password" autocomplete="current-password">
<input type="password" autocomplete="new-password">
